papers.title: Improved constraints on Galactic Centre ejection of hypervelocity stars based on novel search method
papers.author_names: Sill Verberne Elena Maria Rossi Sergey E. Koposov Tommaso Marchetti Konrad Kuijken Zephyr Penoyre Fraser A. Evans Dimitris Souropanis Cl\\\\'ar-Br\\\\'id Tohill

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are stars which have been ejected from the Galactic Centre (GC) at velocities of up to a few thousand km/s. They are tracers of the Galactic potential and can be used to infer properties of the GC, such as the initial-mass function and assembly history. HVSs are rare, however, with only about a dozen promising candidates discovered so far. In this work we make use of a novel, highly efficient method to identify new HVS candidates in Gaia. This method uses the nearly radial trajectories of HVSs to infer their distances and velocities based on their position and Gaia proper motion alone. Through comparison of inferred distances with Gaia parallaxes and photometry we identified 600 HVS candidates with G<20 including the previously discovered S5-HVS1, out of which we obtained ground-based follow-up observations for 196 stars. As we found no new HVSs based on their radial velocity, we used detailed HVS ejection simulations to significantly improve previous HVS ejection rate constraints. In particular, the ejection rate of HVSs more massive than 1 $\\\\mathrm{M_\\\\odot}$ cannot be higher than $10^{-5}$ yr$^{-1}$ at $2\\\\sigma$ significance. Additionally, we predict that there are 5-45 unbound HVSs in the complete Gaia catalogue ($1\\\\sigma$ interval), most of which will be main-sequence stars of a few M$_\\\\odot$ at heliocentric distances of tens to hundreds of kpc. By comparing our results to literature HVS candidates, we find an indication of either a time-dependent ejection rate of HVSs or a non-GC origin of previously identified HVS candidates.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A novel search method leverages the nearly radial trajectories of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) in Gaia DR3 to infer their distances and velocities from position and proper motion alone, identifying 600 HVS candidates with G < 20. Ground-based follow-up confirms 196 stars, but no new HVSs are found via radial velocity; instead, detailed ejection simulations constrain the HVS ejection rate for stars >1 M☉ to ≤10⁻⁵ yr⁻¹ at 2σ, and predict 5–45 unbound HVSs in Gaia’s full catalogue, mostly main-sequence stars of a few M☉ at tens to hundreds of kpc. Results suggest either a time-dependent ejection rate or non-GC origins for previously identified HVS candidates.

source_fields.title: Improved constraints on Galactic Centre ejection of hypervelocity stars based on novel search method

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a machine learning-based approach that uses deep neural networks to detect and characterize exoplanets from transit light curves by learning the complex patterns of brightness dips associated with planetary transits. Appendix#2: Appendix C shows that most Gaia-observable hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are faint, with their G-magnitude distribution peaking in the fainter magnitudes due to the increasing observed volume at fainter levels. Appendix#3: Using a log-uniform prior on $1/\\lambda$ removes the lower limit on the ejection rate, allowing the posterior to peak as the rate approaches zero, suggesting a more physically motivated prior choice. Conclusion: This study establishes robust constraints on the ejection rate and mass function of hypervelocity stars, showing that fewer than 10⁻⁵ yr⁻¹ of such stars were ejected over the past 50–100 Myr, and reveals that many previously identified candidates likely do not originate in the Galactic Center. Discussion: The analysis finds that the ejection rate of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) is constrained to be consistent with simulations under standard assumptions, with key results showing a posterior distribution unaffected by metallicity, Galactic potential, or mass function variations within statistical uncertainty, and the inclusion of the Large Magellanic Cloud having a negligible impact on the inferred ejection rate. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel, highly efficient method using Gaia Data Release 3 to identify and select hypervelocity star candidates ejected from the Galactic Centre, addressing the long-standing problem of in-situ star formation in the extreme tidal environment near Sgr A* by providing robust observational constraints that support a migration mechanism linking HVS progenitors to the S-star cluster. Observations: This section details the observational setup, data reduction, and spectral analysis procedures used to confirm or reject high-velocity star (HVS) candidates through radial velocity measurements. HVS Mock CATALOGUES: This section outlines the use of simulations based on the Hills ejection mechanism to model and constrain the properties of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) in the Galactic center, leveraging a mock catalogue framework to inform candidate selection and environmental constraints. Hvs Candidate Selection: This section presents a method to identify Galactic Center-originating hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by selecting candidates with radial trajectories away from the Galactic Center, enabling distance and radial velocity estimation from Gaia data using only position, proper motion, and known Sun-GC parameters. Constraints On The Galactic Centre: This section establishes the observational limitations of Gaia in detecting HVSs, showing that Gaia is primarily sensitive to massive, main-sequence HVSs at heliocentric distances between 10 and 200 kpc, with completeness decreasing for low-mass stars and being nearly complete for high-mass stars, while the initial mass function has a minimal effect on the evolved-to-main-sequence star ratio.

papers.title: A high-velocity star recently ejected by an intermediate-mass black hole in M15
papers.author_names: Yang Huang (UCAS/NAOC) Qingzheng Li (YNAO/UCAS) Jifeng Liu (NAOC) Xiaobo Dong (YNAO) Huawei Zhang (PKU) Youjun Lu (NAOC) Cuihua Du (UCAS)

abstract.text: The existence of intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) is crucial for understanding various astrophysical phenomena, yet their existence remains elusive, except for the LIGO-Virgo detection. We report the discovery of a high-velocity star J0731+3717, whose backward trajectory about 21 Myr ago intersects that of globular cluster M15 within the cluster tidal radius. Both its metallicity [Fe/H] and its alpha-to-iron abundance ratio [$\\\\alpha$/Fe] are consistent with those of M15. Furthermore, its location falls right on the fiducial sequence of the cluster M15 on the color-absolute magnitude diagram, suggesting similar ages. These support that J0731+3717 is originally associated with M15 at a confidence level of "seven nines". We find that such a high-velocity star ($V_{\\\\rm ej} = 548^{+6}_{-5}$ km s$^{-1}$) was most likely tidally ejected from as close as one astronomical unit to the center of M15, confirming an IMBH ($\\\\ge 100 M_{\\\\odot}$ with a credibility of 98%) as the exclusive nature of the central unseen mass proposed previously.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper reports the discovery of a high-velocity star J0731+3717 ejected from the globular cluster M15, with backward trajectory intersecting the cluster within its tidal radius ~21 Myr ago. Its chemical composition and position on the color-magnitude diagram match M15, supporting a cluster origin at >99% confidence. The ejection velocity (548 km/s) implies a close encounter (<1 AU) with a central massive object, and dynamical modeling confirms a 98% credibility of an intermediate-mass black hole (≥100 M☉) as the sole source of the unseen mass, ruling out stellar remnants.

source_fields.title: A high-velocity star recently ejected by an intermediate-mass black hole in M15

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a stellar and orbital analysis method using Gaia, SDSS, and stellar isochrone data to determine the properties and evolutionary history of stars in globular clusters like M15, leveraging Python-based tools (Gala and galpy) to model their dynamical and stellar characteristics. Conclusion: The detection of high-velocity stars like J0731+3717 ejected by intermediate-mass black holes via the Hills mechanism provides a robust, observable method to identify IMBHs and reveals a promising path for discovering numerous such cases, advancing our understanding of black hole evolution. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel approach to unambiguously identify intermediate-mass black holes in globular clusters by detecting hyper/high-velocity stars ejected during tidal interactions with IMBHs, thereby addressing the key problem of distinguishing IMBHs from massive stellar remnants in the central regions of clusters. References#2: This section presents orbital and stellar population analyses of the star J0731+3717, supporting its origin in the Galactic halo through backward integration, isochrone comparisons, and velocity-metallicity distributions, with results robust across various modeling assumptions. B Systematic Search: A systematic search of 943 high-velocity halo stars using backward orbital integrations reveals J0731+3717 as a candidate ejected from globular cluster M15 within its tidal radius about 21 Myr ago, with a close encounter characterized by a relative velocity of ~541 km s⁻¹ and a distance ratio $d_{\\text{min}}/r_t < 1$, meeting both orbital and metallicity criteria. A Coordinate systems: The section defines a right-handed Cartesian and a Galactocentric cylindrical coordinate system with the Sun located at (8.178, 0.025) kpc and a circular velocity of 220 km/s, along with specified solar motion parameters relative to the local standard of rest. C Orbital deflections: Orbital deflections caused by encounters with field stars are negligible compared to the tidal radius and half-light radius of M15, having minimal impact on the backward orbital analysis. I Black hole mass of M15: The analysis of progenitor binaries in M15, incorporating ejection probabilities and observational constraints, strongly indicates that J0731+3717 was ejected by a black hole of at least 100 $M_{\\odot}$ hosted in the cluster. G Color-absolute magnitude diagram of M15: The color-absolute magnitude diagram of M15 is constructed using precise photometric data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, with member stars selected based on positional, photometric, and quality criteria, and the cluster's fiducial sequence converted to absolute magnitudes using distance and reddening values. E SEGUE spectra and parameters of J0731+3717: The SEGUE spectra of J0731+3717 reveal it as a very metal-poor star with [Fe/H] ≤ −2, and its heliocentric radial velocity is re-estimated as 196.68 ± 6.97 km s⁻¹ using a metal-poor template, confirming its extreme metal-poor nature and providing a robust velocity measurement consistent with independent analyses. D Orbital analysis with alternative assumptions: Comprehensive tests under alternative assumptions show that the backward orbital reconstructions of M15 are robust, with meeting times and closest approach distances remaining consistent and negligible impacts on the results. F Mass, age and improved distance of J0731+3717: Using a Bayesian approach with SDSS photometry, Gaia parallax, and stellar isochrones, the mass, age, and distance of J0731+3717 are determined as $0.69_{-0.01}^{+0.02} \\mathrm{M}_{\\odot}$, $13.00_{-2.00}^{+1.75}$ Gyr, and $1295.2 \\pm 13.1$ pc, respectively, with the age consistent with that of M15. A high-velocity star ejected from globular cluster M15: A backward orbital integration identifies J0731+3717 as a rare high-velocity star likely ejected from the globular cluster M15, with their trajectories intersecting 21 million years ago within M15's tidal radius. H Confidence level of the ejection of J0731+3717 from M15: The combined probability of J0731+3717 being a random star in the halo—due to orbital, chemical, and age coincidences—is only $5.89 \\times 10^{-8}$, confirming it is ejected from M15 with a confidence level of "seven nines." A high-velocity star recently ejected from the globular cluster M15: This section reports the discovery of a high-velocity star ejected from the globular cluster M15, likely due to dynamical interactions within the cluster. A high-velocity star recently ejected by an intermediate-mass black hole in M15: A high-velocity star has been identified as recently ejected by an intermediate-mass black hole in the globular cluster M15. J Estimate the number of J0731+3717 like star in the searching volume of current and future surveys: This section estimates that approximately 50 to 540 J0731+3717-like high-velocity stars are ejected from globular clusters within 5–10 kpc of the Sun over the past 14 Gyr via the Hills mechanism, consistent with dynamical simulations and significantly exceeding rates from single-binary interactions.

We find that such a high-velocity star ($V_{\\\\rm ej} = 548^{+6}_{-5}$ km s$^{-1}$) was most likely tidally ejected from as close as one astronomical unit to the center of M15, confirming an IMBH ($\\
title: A high-velocity star recently ejected by an intermediate-mass black hole in M15

catalog_assessment.evidence: Reports discovery of specific high-velocity star J0731+3717 with detailed observational data: trajectory, metallicity, abundance ratios, color-magnitude diagram position, and ejection velocity measurements.

papers.title: Spectroscopic and Dynamic Orbital Analyses of Metal-Poor and High Proper Motion Stars: I. HD\\\\,8724 and HD\\\\,195633
papers.author_names: M. Marismak T. Sahin F. Güney O. Plevne S. Bilir

abstract.text: In this study, spectral, age, kinematic, and orbital dynamical analyses were conducted on metal-poor and high proper-motion (HPM) stars, HD 8724 and HD 195633, selected from the Solar neighborhood. This analysis combines detailed abundance measurements, kinematics, and orbital dynamics to determine their origin. Standard 1D local thermodynamic equilibrium analysis provides a fresh determination of the atmospheric parameters: $T_{\\\\rm eff}=$4700$\\\\pm$115 K, $\\\\log g=$ 1.65$\\\\pm$0.32 cgs, [Fe/H]=-1.59$\\\\pm$0.04 dex, and a microturbulent velocity $\\\\xi=$ 1.58$\\\\pm$0.50 km s$^{\\\\rm -1}$ for HD 8724 and $T_{\\\\rm eff}=$6100$\\\\pm$205 K, $\\\\log g=$3.95$\\\\pm$0.35 cgs, [Fe/H]=-0.52$\\\\pm$0.05 dex, and $\\\\xi=$1.26$\\\\pm$0.50 km s$^{\\\\rm -1}$ for HD 195633. The ages were estimated using a Bayesian approach (12.25 Gyr for HD 8724 and 8.15 Gyr for HD 195633). The escape scenarios of these stars from 170 candidate globular clusters (GCs) in the Galaxy were also investigated because of their chemical and physical differences (HPM and metal-poor nature). Accordingly, the calculated probability of encounter ($59\\\\%$) for HD 8724 at a distance of five tidal radius suggests that star HD 8724 may have escaped from NGC 5139 ($\\\\omega$ Cen), supported by its highly flattened orbit and may belong to a sub-population of this GC. Conversely, HD 195633's kinematics, age, and metal abundances point towards an escape from the bulge GC NGC 6356.

source_fields.sections_text: Footnotes: The footnotes provide citations, corrections, and sources for radial velocity calculations, abundance uncertainties, and references to databases and software used in the study. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel combination of high-resolution spectroscopic analysis, Bayesian age estimation, and kinematic orbital modeling of F-type metal-poor stars to refine their population classification and Galactic origin, addressing the problem of ambiguities in identifying and interpreting the formation histories of ancient stellar systems. | Age Determination: The ages of HD 8724 and HD 195633 were determined using Bayes statistics to compare their atmospheric parameters with PARSEC isochrones, yielding most probable ages of 12.25 ± 0.58 Gyr and 8.15 ± 1.40 Gyr, respectively. | Kinematic Analyses: The study calculated the space velocities of HD 8724 and HD 195633 using Gaia data and radial velocities, applying corrections for differential rotation and the local standard of rest to derive accurate Galactic orbital parameters. | Spectroscopic Data: Spectroscopic data from the ELODIE library, processed with the LIME code for continuum normalization and line identification, confirm consistent radial velocities with Gaia DR3 measurements and enable precise equivalent width analysis using atomic data from NIST and VALD. | Model Atmosphere Parameters And Abundances: The paper proposes a local thermodynamic equilibrium (LTE) approach using the ATLAS9 model atmospheres and the MOOG line analysis code to determine stellar parameters—effective temperature, surface gravity, microturbulence, and metallicity—by analyzing neutral and ionized iron lines, with microturbulence derived from the independence of iron abundances on line excitation potential and reduced equivalent width. | Dynamical Orbital Analyses, Galactic Origins Of Stars And Concluding Remarks: This section demonstrates that high-proper-motion metal-poor stars like HD 8724 and HD 195633 likely originated from globular clusters through dynamical encounters, as evidenced by orbital simulations showing their proximity within five tidal radii of approximately 170 GCs over the last 13 billion years.
